smartlead_campaign: OR #3 | Ecom Leads | Scroll Stopper | Personlization

magnet.report_label: Scroll-Stopper Sheet (the 3 ads we wrote them)

thread.subject: something for LPC
thread.body_text:
hey Leilani, noticed LPC Golf's founder never touched leather or a sewing machine before starting, curious how that came about.

on a separate note: I built 3 meta ad mockups from LPC's own products, plus a world-class brand playbook from your site and reviews.

 no pitch attached, it's already built.

 should I send it?

- Kyle

 P.S. you gotta show people what an amazing brand you got for real!!

thread.subject: RE: something for LPC
thread.body_text:
Leilani - the part most brands react to isn't even the ads, it's the brand playbook. it lays out your voice, the exact language your customers use in reviews, and what you can and can't say in ad copy.

it's all set to send alongside the mockups. want me to send both over?

- Kyle

I am open to a conversation.  

 Best, 
Leilani

Love it - as promised in my first email, here are your 3 ad mockups built from LPC's own products, plus your brand playbook. 

 the mockups show the angles, the playbook is the part most brands actually keep - your voice, the words your customers use, and what you can and can't say in ad copy.

 if any of them click, the quickest way to see how I'd actually run them is a 30 min call. → Calendar 

 - Kyle

thread.subject: Re: RE: something for LPC
thread.body_text:
Hi Kyle,

I've booked some time on our calendar for Monday.

I had one quick piece of feedback on the mockups: the golf bag in image 2 appears way too small in comparison to the rest of the objects, but the others look great.

Also, just as a heads-up, our site is a custom Hydrogen build. I'm a software engineer, so I can implement changes myself, but please note that it is not a standard Shopify template.

Best,
Leilani

Leilani - fair call on the bag scale. the mockups were built from public site assets only, so think of them as angle concepts - with your actual product files they get a lot sharper. good problem to have.

 Hydrogen build is no issue at all, and having a software engineer on the brand side actually speeds everything up.

 talk Monday :)

 - Kyle

copy.tldr: LPC makes premium handmade leather golf headcovers in San Francisco, they run no ads today and have almost no visible social proof, so this is a build-from-zero play that starts with the one Scroll-Stopper ad that shows the craft up close.

deep_dive.what_they_are: A small craft brand called LPC Golf (legal name Little Prince Customs). Handcrafted golf headcovers, hand-stitched by one artisan named Karl, made in San Francisco. Founder reportedly had never worked with leather or a sewing machine before starting. Site is a custom Hydrogen build, and Leilani the contact is a software engineer who can make site changes herself.
deep_dive.what_they_sell: Premium customizable leather golf headcovers. Three collections named on the site: Quilted (20 designs, hand-sewn diamond patterns), Exotic Classic (2 designs, embossed cowhide), and Artisan (8 designs, signature and rare-material pieces). They offer custom embroidery like initials, logos, or designs. Do not quote any prices, none are public.
deep_dive.positioning: The heritage-craft, premium end of the golf accessory market. Artisan and personalized, not mass-produced. Built to last, made for you.
deep_dive.who_buys: Discerning golfers who care about craftsmanship and personal style, and will pay more for a handmade leather cover that stands out on the course and lasts. Likely gift buyers too, given the custom embroidery angle.
customer_voice: These headcovers are absolutely amazing! The craftsmanship is top-notch and I get compliments on them every time I'm on the course.
strengths: A real, ownable story: one artisan, made in the USA, hand-stitched. That is rare in a category full of factory product and it makes strong ad creative.
Customization (embroidery, build-a-set) gives a natural reason to buy more than one and a built-in gifting angle.
gaps: Almost no visible social proof. Only one testimonial on the whole site and no review count. Cold traffic needs proof before it buys a premium item.
No ads running now and no scrapable catalog or prices, so we are starting from zero and cannot yet model revenue with any confidence.

game_plan.headline: Lead with the craft story to a warm-ish golf audience, prove one product can sell cold, then widen and add a gifting angle.

phases.goal: Prove that a cold golfer will buy one of these covers from an ad at all. Learn which angle pulls.
campaigns: One sales campaign, one main audience (broad golf interest plus lookalike of any existing customer or email list if they have one).
Test the 3 Scroll-Stopper ads against each other so we learn the winning angle fast.
lead_products: Lead with the Quilted collection. It has 20 designs, by far the most of any collection, which almost always means it is the range they sell most and are most confident making. Wide choice also means we can match an ad to more buyers. Feature their single strongest-selling Quilted cover once Leilani confirms which one that is.
phases.creative: Run the 3 ads from the Scroll-Stopper sheet. Go live first with the ad that shows the hand-stitching and leather up close, because craft is their one clear edge and it is what the review praises. Hold the ad with the small golf bag (image 2) until we rebuild it with their real product files, since Leilani already flagged the scale looks off. That fix comes fast because she can send us the assets.
phases.budget: Start small and steady so we can read results, not scale yet.
phases.what_success_looks_like: One ad angle clearly beats the others and brings in first orders at a cost the margin can carry. We do not chase volume this month.

phases.goal: Put more money behind the winning angle and widen who sees it.
campaigns: Scale spend on the winning ad and audience from month one.
Add a retargeting campaign for site visitors and add-to-carts, leaning on the custom embroidery and build-a-set-save-10% offers to close them.
lead_products: Keep Quilted as the hero. Introduce the Artisan collection (8 designs) as the step-up, higher-craft option for buyers who want something rarer.
phases.creative: Add 1 to 2 new ads built around the winning angle. Start collecting and using real customer photos and quotes in ads to fix the social proof gap.
phases.budget: Raise spend gradually, only on what already worked in month one.
phases.what_success_looks_like: The winner holds its return at higher spend, and retargeting starts closing people who did not buy the first time.

phases.goal: Scale the proven setup and add a second product line.
campaigns: Scale the winning campaign further with fresh creative to fight ad fatigue.
Launch a gifting push for the custom-embroidery angle (initials, logos), aimed at people buying for a golfer.
lead_products: Add the Exotic Classic collection (embossed cowhide) as a premium statement piece to sell to the buyers we now know convert. Second product only comes in once the first is proven.
phases.creative: Refresh the best ads and add a gift-focused set. Use any reviews gathered in months 1 and 2 as proof.
phases.budget: Scale in line with what the return supports, not faster.
phases.what_success_looks_like: A repeatable winning ad plus a gifting angle both returning above breakeven, so spend can keep climbing safely.

estimates.label: Starting budget
estimates.value: Around $50 to $75 a day to start
estimates.basis: enough to gather a few hundred clicks across the first weeks so we can tell which of the 3 ads works, without spending big before anything is proven. Premium single-item price means we need patience, not volume, early on.

estimates.label: Revenue we would aim to add
estimates.value: We cannot give an honest number yet
estimates.basis: no prices, order value, or past ad results are public, and they have never run ads. Guessing here would be the one thing that loses the deal on the call. Once Leilani shares average order value and margin, we can model a real target.

assumptions: Quilted is their bestseller because it has the most designs. If Leilani says a different collection sells most, we lead with that instead.
The price and margin are high enough on a handmade leather cover to carry paid ads. If the order value is low, the whole spend plan changes.
They have some existing customers or an email list we can build a lookalike from. If not, month one leans fully on cold interest targeting and moves slower.
The image 2 bag-scale issue is fixable quickly once they send real product files.

questions.question: Have you ever run Meta ads before, even a boosted post? And is anyone running any paid ads for you right now?
questions.listening_for: We found none live. Confirm they are truly starting from zero, or find out something stopped. If they tried and it flopped, ask what happened before promising anything.

questions.question: Roughly what does one of your headcovers sell for, and what does it cost you to make one?
questions.listening_for: This is the number that decides if ads can work at all. A high price with healthy margin carries paid ads. If the margin is thin, be honest that the plan changes.

questions.question: What is a comfortable amount for you to put toward testing ads each month while we learn?
questions.listening_for: Whether $50 to $75 a day is realistic for them. If budget is very tight, set expectations that learning takes longer.

questions.question: Who would be my day-to-day contact, and can you send product photos and files quickly when I ask?
questions.listening_for: Leilani is a software engineer and can make site changes, which is a big plus. Confirm she can turn around assets fast, since the plan depends on it.

questions.question: Which collection or single cover sells the most today, and which do you make the most money on?
questions.listening_for: Confirms or breaks my Quilted-is-the-bestseller assumption. Their answer decides which product we lead with.

questions.question: How many past customers or email subscribers do you have that we could build an audience from?
questions.listening_for: A list means we can start with a warmer audience and move faster. No list means pure cold targeting and slower learning.

questions.question: I only saw one testimonial on the site. Do you have more reviews, customer photos, or messages we could use?
questions.listening_for: This is the biggest gap for ads. If they have proof sitting in DMs or email, we can use it. If they truly have almost none, gathering it becomes part of the job.

questions.question: When someone lands on your site, how many actually buy? And does the custom order process ever slow people down?
questions.listening_for: Whether the problem is traffic or the site itself. If good traffic already does not convert, sending more paid clicks wastes money until we fix the site or offer.

questions.question: How long does a custom headcover take to make and ship?
questions.listening_for: One artisan hand-making everything may mean long lead times. If it takes weeks, that must be clear in ads or buyers get frustrated and refund.

questions.question: Do you get many people finding you by searching things like 'custom golf headcovers' or 'leather headcovers'?
questions.listening_for: People clearly search for golf headcovers, so there is a real Google Search opening here. Listen for whether they already show up or are invisible on search. This is an honest add-on, not a stretch.

questions.question: How much of your business comes from gifts, and do you see spikes around the holidays or Father's Day?
questions.listening_for: Custom embroidery makes this a natural gift. Strong gift demand supports both the month-3 gifting push and seasonal Google campaigns.

objections.objection: We have never run ads. How do I know these will even work?
objections.answer: You do not yet, and that is exactly why month one is small and about learning, not scaling. We run your 3 ads against each other on a modest budget and let real buyers tell us which angle works before you spend more. You are buying proof, not promises.

objections.objection: Each cover is handmade and takes time. I can't handle a flood of orders.
objections.answer: Good, because the plan does not create a flood. We start slow on purpose and only raise spend as you prove you can keep up. Your lead time actually goes in the ads as part of the craft story, so buyers expect it and value it.

objections.objection: My product is premium and personal. Ads will make it look cheap.
objections.answer: Agreed, so we lead with the craft, the leather, and the hand-stitching, not discounts. The playbook already sets what we will and will not say. The whole point is to show people how good this brand really is, which is what you told me yourself.

objections.objection: I only have one review on my site. Isn't that a problem for ads?
objections.answer: It is honestly the biggest thing to fix, and I would rather say that than pretend otherwise. Cold buyers need proof before paying a premium. So part of month one and two is gathering your existing customer photos and messages and putting them to work. If you have more than what is on the site, we are in good shape.

red_flags.flag: Almost no visible social proof. Only one testimonial and no review count anywhere on the site.
red_flags.why_it_matters: People do not spend premium money on an unknown brand cold without proof. We may need to gather reviews before ads can perform, which slows early results.

red_flags.flag: No prices, no catalog, and no past ad data are public.
red_flags.why_it_matters: We cannot honestly model revenue or return yet. Kyle must not throw out any dollar figure on the call. Get order value and margin first.

red_flags.flag: Everything is hand-made by one artisan, Karl.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Production may not scale fast. If ads work too well, long lead times could cause refund requests and bad word of mouth. Scaling must stay tied to what he can produce.

red_flags.flag: Custom items cannot be returned, and the customer pays return shipping.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Since most orders are custom, buyers take on more risk. That makes strong proof and clear ad messaging even more important so expectations are set upfront.

other_channel_openings: Google Search ads. Golfers actively search for terms like 'custom golf headcovers' and 'leather golf headcovers', so there is real existing demand to capture, not a category we would have to invent.

SEO. A brand with a genuine made-in-USA, hand-stitched story and named collections can rank over time for those same search terms, which lowers reliance on paid clicks. Worth raising only after confirming they show up poorly today.
